data.title: Turning Geopolitical Noise Into Opportunity: 3 Buy-The-Dip Stocks
data.description:
Headline Noise: A Short-Term Distraction from Top Growth Stocks

Geopolitical headlines continue to inject uncertainty into global markets with renewed U.S. tensions involving Greenland’s sovereignty, while Iran and Venezuela have also weighed on investor sentiment. Some company-specific headlines are also cropping up as earnings season begins to focus more on tech and AI-related companies. Historically, these market events tend to pressure stocks perceived as expensive, cyclical, or tied to global growth, even when company fundamentals remain intact. The result is often short-term multiple compression rather than lasting impairment. As markets are held back by risk-off sentiment, many growth stocks with strong profitability and forward earnings have pulled back along with weaker names. This creates opportunity.

Slowing Momentum Presents Opportunity for Select Stocks

The following three stocks, all of which are current holdings in my Alpha Picks and among my Top 10 AI Stocks for 2026, are supported by superior growth and profitability metrics, yet have recently pulled back amid geopolitical uncertainty and other headline noise. For investors willing to look beyond superficial distraction, these dips may represent attractive entry points before fundamentals return to the forefront of investor focus.

How I Chose Three "Buy-the-Dip" Stocks

The three stocks I chose to highlight buy-the-dip opportunities amid the Greenland focus, geopolitical uncertainty, and headline noise are companies I know intimately because they're holdings in my Alpha Picks portfolio, and I’ve covered them in premium articles in recent weeks. Since I follow their performance and I know their growth and profitability stories, I can see that recent downside pressure on these stocks may present short-term buying opportunities.

1. Celestica Inc. (CLS)

  • Market Capitalization: $34.90B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/26/2026): 14 out of 534

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/26/2026): 2 out of 18

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Electronic Manufacturing Services

Celestica is a global electronics manufacturing services provider delivering off-the-shelf or customized solutions for networking, storage, and computing. Shares have pulled back amid headlines that have investors reassessing valuations of AI-focused companies like CLS. However, Celestica’s fundamentals, which are supported by favorable Quant Factors, remain compelling. The company is a beneficiary of AI server demand, which supports steadily expanding margins.

I start with Celestica because it has headline challenges beyond the geopolitical noise, and it delivers its Q4 earnings this Thursday before the market opens. CLS shares sank nearly 8% on Thursday after Digitimes reported that Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) tensor processing unit servers may be assembled by a supplier other than Celestica. The news is still a rumor at this point, and the potential negative impact, if any, has not been confirmed. Looking beyond the superficial headline noise, Celestica remains well positioned within broader AI data center infrastructure spending and is projected to deliver 27% revenue growth in CY25, and management is expected to reaffirm continued growth from strong client relationships at its earnings call on Thursday. From there, the peer-leading performance momentum may resume.

CLS 1-Year Performance Momentum vs. Peers

As headline concerns fade and enterprise visibility comes back into focus, Celestica's earnings power and profitability could help resume its recent momentum. This sets the stage for our next featured stock, which is an AI infrastructure beneficiary.

2. Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO)

  • Market Capitalization: $24.05B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/26/2026): 15 out of 534

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/26/2026): 3 out of 69

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

Credo Technology designs high-speed connectivity solutions critical to modern data centers, enabling faster and more energy-efficient data transmission. This distinction won it a position in my short list of Best AI Stocks For 2026 Data Center Growth that I published last week. The stock has recently declined alongside other semiconductor stocks perceived as overpriced and thus more sensitive to short-term market concerns. Yet Credo’s growth outlook remains tightly linked to the AI-driven data center buildout, where demand for its high bandwidth, low-latency GPU-to-GPU connectivity appears to remain strong and potentially accelerating in 2026. As I pointed out last week, Credo’s superficial valuation metrics are not a concern when looking deeper into growth.

With a forward PEG comfortably below 1.00 and offering more than 50% discount to the sector median, CRDO looks attractive when looking beneath the surface of the P/E. To support this valuation perspective, we dig deeper into the growth metrics. Forward EBITDA growth is expected at 267.19%, which is more than 20 times the sector median, while CRDO’s long-term growth (3-5-year CAGR), at 68.53%, is more than four times the sector.

As geopolitical noise subsides and investors refocus on earnings durability beyond superficial headlines, Credo’s differentiated technology and profitability trajectory could reassert leadership, leading naturally into a software-driven growth story.

3. AppLovin (APP)

  • Market Capitalization: $177.23B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 01/26/2026):47 out of 534

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 01/26/2026): 5 out of 76

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Application Software

AppLovin offers a suite of marketing technologies that enables businesses to acquire customers, monetize them, and measure the performance of their advertising efforts. The stock has experienced volatility, and like Celestica, AppLovin recently faced negative headline impact beyond macro and geopolitical news. APP’s stock declined 5% last Tuesday amid a short report from CapitalWatch. Meanwhile, AppLovin’s fundamentals tell a different story as its AXON AI platform continues to support strong revenue growth and expanding margins.

APP’s forward revenue growth is expected at more than 33%, which is more than four times the sector median, while its 62% forward EBITDA growth is nearly five times higher than its sector peers. Like our other two featured stocks, this forward growth expectation justifies its premium valuation as APP’s PEG is 1.04, offering a 37% discount to the sector. AppLovin’s sector-crushing EBITDA margin signals that its AI-driven advertising platform is not just growing but doing so with superior operating efficiency and pricing power compared to peers. Strong cash from operations reinforces that growth is translating into cash generation that gives AppLovin flexibility to reinvest aggressively in AI, reduce leverage, or return capital without relying on outside funding.

As geopolitical tensions calm and downside pressure from negative headlines recedes, advertising budgets tend to normalize, often benefiting platforms, such as AppLovin's, with proven performance benefits. For investors willing to buy through volatility, AppLovin, as well as Celestica and Credo, exemplify profitable companies that can rise again following sentiment-driven pullbacks.

Conclusion: Opportunity Arises Out of Headline Noise

Geopolitical uncertainty and headline noise have once again created potential dislocations between price and fundamentals. Celestica, Credo Technology Group, and AppLovin each reflect a similar pattern of short-term pressure driven by risk-off sentiment, rather than deteriorating business conditions. While the potential for further volatility remains, and investors should proceed with caution, these companies all exhibit durable growth, improving profitability, and exposure to long-term secular trends. As history has shown, when geopolitical noise fades, fundamentals tend to reclaim the primary focus for investors, rewarding those willing to take added risk during periods of uncertainty.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you're furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!

data.title: Global Turmoil: Why Gold, Oil, And Semiconductors Are Back In Focus
data.description:
News From the Weekend

This has been one of the more eventful past weeks during the current Trump administration. The Davos conclave concluded with some semblance of clarity on what the fate of Greenland might be. A U.S. aircraft carrier strike group arrived off the shore of Iran as IRGC ground personnel scramble. China had a political upheaval, and Cuba is also becoming a possible blockade target, similar to what preempted activities in Venezuela. The market (VOO, IVV, DIA, QQQ, VTWO) continues to stabilize after the tariff talk at the Davos outset subsides. Let's review.

China Has Another Political Shake-up

In what is being called the biggest CCP political arrest since 1971 [When Mao Zedong moved to strip Lin Biao of his power over the military], General Zhang Youxia, considered second in command to Xi Jinping in regard to the military and possibly the country, was purged. The majority of the PLA military command has now been purged or demoted for various charges. In the case of Zhang, the charge officially is leaking Chinese nuclear secrets to U.S. intelligence. While some are skeptical of the charge, believing it was levied to be serious enough to stick, what's done is done, and Xi Jinping has further consolidated power.

One positive catalyst from this may be related to Taiwan and the possible delay of any possibility of a Chinese invasion. From the outside, the PLA now appears to be fractionalized and not all on the same page. Leadership positions may not be as desirable now with the series of purges that have occurred, and convincing those that are capable below the general rank to move up in command may be difficult. The reason why the arrest of Zhang Youxia was a big deal is that he was considered a close ally of Xi and widely respected. This is an important matter now making its way through Western media headlines.

The Abraham Lincoln Has Arrived

Many were counting the days until a new Iran strike during the middle days of the Iranian protests. President Trump criticized the Iranian regime and pronounced that the U.S. would not stand for the killings of Iranian protestors. A strike never happened, and part of this may have been due to surrounding allies not wanting to be retaliatory targets:

The UAE on Monday said it would not allow its airspace, territory, or waters to be used in any hostile military actions against Iran, after a U.S. aircraft carrier arrived in the Middle East.

The USS Abraham Lincoln has now arrived, and the U.S. has a launching point should it decide to proceed with strikes. Any time the Strait of Hormuz gets attention, oil and energy stocks come into focus.

Cuba is on Watch

Last week, the Trump administration was quoted as now considering an oil blockade on Cuba. The belief is that the country was so reliant on Venezuelan-sanctioned oil imports that merely cutting that off could mean an end to the government:

The Trump administration is weighing new tactics to drive regime change in Cuba, including imposing a total blockade on oil imports to the Caribbean country, three people familiar with the plan said Thursday.

The main catalysts, as they relate to energy markets here, are different from those of Iran and the surrounding passageways. The U.S. is doing everything it can to enforce oil sanctions. Many countries had been beneficiaries of black market oil that was traded either at a lower price [since the sanctioned country could not sell into the normal market] or as trade for other services. Forcing more buyers of sanctioned oil back into the normal market would mean more buyers at market prices.

Greenland in Negotiation

As meetings in Davos ended, President Trump seemed to indicate a deal for "total access" to Greenland. What that means is hard to say. The U.S. does currently have a coastal base but has largely stayed away from the interior of Greenland since the 1960s, when the U.S. was building underground bases. The project was called Camp Century and was abandoned after shifting ice over the rooftops made maintenance unfeasible. The project also left behind modular nuclear reactors, which are now being exposed after the recent ice melt.

Denmark's leadership might not be on the same page as NATO:

Leaders of Denmark and Greenland insisted Thursday that the island's sovereignty was non-negotiable after U.S. President Donald Trump said he had agreed with the NATO chief on the framework of a future Arctic security deal that Trump said would grant the U.S. "total access" to the territory.

Gold & Silver

Looking into what asset classes and ETFs might gain further traction from the recent machinations of world government shifts, gold and silver are not losing momentum. Popular gold ETFs like the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) and The Goldman Sachs Physical Gold ETF (AAAU) have delivered market-beating returns over the last 12 months. This is one of those situations where nothing happens for years, and then everything happens all at once. Silver, shown by the silver proxy ETF, the iShares Silver Trust (SLV), has been an even more extreme catch-up trade.

One thing I know about China from working in the country for a decade is that Chinese investors love two asset classes, real estate and gold. Real estate is in a serious bear market in China right now, leaving only one true desirable store of value asset in gold. Not just for China but for any country around the world, if they sense instability that may lead to reduced trade and foreign investment outflow, a logical hedge against their currency being traded and held less and less [and thus depreciating] is to buy gold and possibly silver.

I also am of the personal belief that as the blockchain and instant settlement get closer and closer to a reality for publicly traded securities, gold and silver will become digital assets backed by metals. This shouldn't be much different than the digital currency/store of value argument used for Bitcoin (BTC-USD). The only difference is that the shares of a GLD or SLV fund that can eventually be converted into cash or possibly even traded for goods if put on the blockchain are that these funds are backed by gold and silver.

I made that argument in this article: GBTC Vs. GLD: What Is The Real 'Digital Gold' In A 'Risk Off' Market?

Since then, Gold has functioned as the better store of value in volatile times.

Oil

Oil is an interesting subject right now. Many pundits believe we are due for a commodities supercycle, and oil and gas will benefit, with the world theoretically short on all types of energy as data centers multiply. The capture of Maduro in Venezuela was thought of as a seminal moment where investors may anticipate a flood of crude to enter the market. The reaction after has actually seen oil prices rise:

For the most part, the last 12 months have seen outperformance by refiners, as shown here by the VanEck Oil Refiners ETF (CRAK) vs. the Vanguard sector ETF for Energy (VDE).

With oil moving in the opposite direction of what was once anticipated, the market may be seeing something else. As the U.S. has been seizing sanctioned U.S., Venezuelan, and Iranian "ghost ships" that deliver sanctioned crude to those willing to buy it, this black market may be coming to an end. If sanctioned oil is really cut off, end users will have to reenter the crude market at prices closer to $60 rather than $20 and/or without a price for barter deals.

In essence, that black market oil was hurting the prices for lawful sellers, as there was less demand for their product. This demand will now slowly return. Integrated oil funds like a VDE might be a catch-up trade to refiners should the price of oil continue to rise.

Semiconductors and the Taiwan Strait

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) is possibly an even more important semiconductor name than Nvidia (NVDA). They make the most advanced chips for nearly every important player in the industry. They have been building U.S. plants and recently announced a $165 billion expansion into the U.S. Not only has currying favor with the Trump administration and the U.S. been seen as protective to the island of Taiwan, but the Chinese PLA leadership shakeup now has many analysts questioning if a Chinese consolidation of the island is realistic at all on previously thought of timelines.

This puts the world's most important semiconductor fabrication company on possibly a more solid footing than before. The stock was undervalued mostly for its political risk, not the company's operations or profitability. For those who were worried about the Taiwan Strait situation, the recent goings-on in China might now make the company investable for those who had reservations previously.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: YETI: Don't Hold This Stock As Core U.S. Market Wanes
data.description:
2026 is proving to be quite a volatile year so far in the stock market, with investors reacting sharply to the latest volley of political headlines, especially the threat of new tariffs on Europe. Amid the choppiness, I continue to encourage investors to keep our heads down and prepare for a rotation in market leadership, away from large-cap tech stocks and into more value-oriented small and mid-cap names.

Yet in this space, stock selection is key. YETI (YETI) is a stock I once thought attractive, but beginning midway through last year, I began trimming my position as the company's sales waned, especially in the United States. Tariff pressures certainly don't make the fundamental story any easier here. And yet, despite ongoing challenges, YETI has enjoyed a bit of a resurgence, with its stock up more than 20% over the past year. The key question for investors now is: Can YETI's rebound continue?

I last wrote a "Sell" article on YETI in October, when the stock was trading at $33 per share. I'll fully admit upfront that my "Sell" call on YETI was ill-timed and caused me to lose out on the stock's rapid gains since last fall. Investors in particular responded well to the company's guidance boost, which came alongside a re-acceleration in top-line growth. That said, I'm concerned about the company's persistently weakened performance in the United States, and with the latest rally, a lot of YETI's valuation discount has vanished, making a bet on further upside quite challenging. I reiterate my "Sell" rating here.

The next major catalyst for YETI will be its Q4 earnings, expected in early to mid-February. There are two major things I expect in the Q4 update that I think may bring downside risk to the stock:

  • Recent acceleration in international sales may be short-lived, which may threaten the stock's recent momentum narrative
  • The company's profit outlook for FY26 may disappoint, especially as the company faces a full-year burden of tariffs in FY26 (versus only a partial year in FY25)

In my view, it's best to lock in recent gains on this stock and sell out of this position before then.

International Sales Bet is Risky, as Core U.S. Market Falters

Let's now dig into some of the core themes that have driven upside for YETI recently. We have to acknowledge, of course, the company's recent guidance increase.

YETI is now expecting 1%-2% adjusted sales growth in FY25 (adjusted for the fact that this year FY25 benefits from a 53-week fiscal year, versus a standard 52-week year in FY24), versus a wider 0%-2% range previously. Its FCF is now expected at the higher $200 million end of its prior range, while midpoint EPS rises ~1% to $2.44.

These aren't landmark increases, but they do signal a bit of a turnaround for YETI, which in Q3 also returned to a slight 2% sales growth in Q3, versus a 4% decline in Q2.

As shown in the chart above, both wholesale and DTC channels returned to growth in Q3, whereas wholesale was a major drag at a 7% y/y decline in Q2. And yet, there are risks underneath the hood. In Q3, YETI's sales in the United States declined 1% y/y, while international sales grew 14% y/y.

YETI is banking a lot on expanding its mix of international sales. Today, international revenue only represents 20% of YETI's sales mix, versus ~40% or higher for many of its core activewear/consumer products brand peers:

And yet we have to ask a pertinent question: how long can YETI sustain double-digit international sales growth? The company attributed its double-digit international growth to strength and rising popularity in Europe, plus strong demand from the brand's launch in Japan, which kicked off in Q2 of 2025.

We'll tackle the easier one first. Japan will be a difficult comp for YETI in 2026, as the company laps the launch midway through the year. It's highly likely that the sales burst at launch was due to pent-up demand from Japanese consumers, a tailwind that may not repeat in 2026.

Next, there's Europe to consider. It wouldn't be an understatement to say that U.S.-Europe relations have deteriorated substantially over the past year, with Trump's repeated threats over Greenland and escalating tariff pressures. Boycotts of U.S. products, which were prominent in Canada over the past year after Trump's calls to make Canada the 51st state, have also begun to take root in Europe. YETI could be caught in this crossfire, especially as its products are largely commoditized offerings with dozens of local-market alternatives.

Meanwhile, YETI's core U.S. sales have declined for two straight quarters: down 5% y/y in Q2 and down 1% y/y in Q3. With many sources pointing to rather tepid growth in retail sales over the critical holiday period, I expect negative trends in the United States to continue in Q4. Meanwhile, YETI's overall drinkware category is down 4% y/y in Q3, which I also expect to be weak in Q4. To combat sales declines, YETI is turning to new products, including a new shaker bottle; but overall, we have to recognize that YETI is a premium brand offering rather bland household products, and in a tougher consumer economy, people may hang onto their tumblers and water bottles for longer rather than spend unnecessary money to buy a new one.

Margin Compression is Likely to Continue

While YETI's international sales have helped the company return to sales growth, its earnings are actually declining. The chart below showcases that in Q3, YETI's adjusted EPS fell 14% y/y to $0.61, while adjusted operating income dollars also fell 16% y/y to $67 million.

The root of the problem is weaker gross margins, which fell 230 bps y/y to 55.9% and explain the majority of the company's 290 bps y/y decline in operating margins. Of course, it's easy for the company to blame the external pressure of tariffs here (again, we remind investors that YETI will face a full-year burden of tariffs in 2026, versus only a partial year in 2025).

Management notes that especially in the struggling Drinkware category, the competitive market has turned very promotion-intensive. Per CEO Matt Reintjes' remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Within Drinkware, the strength of our core portfolio and our uptempo focus on innovation is driving accelerated momentum despite ongoing wholesale inventory pressure and promotional intensity in the U.S. market. Even as overall sell-in was down year-over-year in the U.S. wholesale, sell-through strength highlights the underlying momentum of YETI, in particular, the durability of our Drinkware business in that highly contested market [...]

In the U.S. wholesale channel, strong C&E performance was offset by a decline in the Drinkware category, stemming from a continuation of trends seen in the second quarter, elevated promotional intensity, coupled with conservative ordering from some of our wholesale partners.

This is a burgeoning trend that investors cannot ignore. Wholesalers stuck with slow-moving inventory will be pressured to put items on clearance to reset their shelves during and after the holiday season. This promotional pressure will limit YETI's ability to sell its product at full price, and to offset the margin burden from tariffs. In other words, we expect downward margin pressure to continue in Q4 and onward in 2026. Meanwhile, the company's corporate overhead is growing (SG&A rose 3% y/y in nominal dollars, and 50 bps as a percentage of sales), when we think the company should be doing more to streamline operations and achieve efficiency in a tougher retail landscape.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

For FY26, Wall Street analysts are currently expecting YETI to generate $2.85 in pro forma EPS, or a rather aggressive expectation for 16% earnings growth on 4% revenue growth. Given current margin pressures, we think it will be tough for YETI to achieve double-digit earnings growth next year, even if the company has been diligently retiring shares via generous buybacks.

Still, even if we take the current Street consensus at face value, current share prices near $47 put YETI at a 16.6x FY26 P/E - not exactly a bargain, despite the risk of weak U.S. sales, the possibility of an international slowdown, and undeniable pressures on YETI's gross margin. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Pan American Silver: Get Ready For A Potential Q4 Earnings Blowout
data.description:
It is no surprise that the stock of Canada-based Pan American Silver Corp. (PAAS) has ripped higher along with the prices of silver and gold. In my opinion, the rally in precious metals has been driven by two primary factors: the ~10% yoy decline in the U.S. dollar and the significant uncertainty introduced to global markets by the Trump administration's arguably chaotic, erratic, and seemingly never-ending tariff, trade, social, and foreign policies. More specifically, President Trump's speech at Davos in which he questioned the EU's commitment to NATO based on false assertions about the alliance's past contributions. The reactions from European leaders were quick, strong, and directly to the point and led many analysts to wonder if Europeans would begin selling the trillions of U.S. Treasuries they own. The result was as you might expect: yet another huge rally in the prices of silver and gold, and—of course - a continuation of the huge rally in the mining stocks.

As you can see from the chart below, the price of silver (as represented by the (SLV) ETF) jumped 14.7% for the week while PAAS stock soared 16.9%. Today, with spot silver closing above $100/oz on Friday, I'll take a look at Pan American Silver to see if it is a buy, sell, or hold.

Investment Thesis

Pan American Silver Corp. is headquartered in Vancouver, Canada, and engages in the exploration, development, extraction, processing, and refining of silver and gold in North and South America: Canada, Mexico, Peru, Bolivia, Argentina, Chile, and Brazil. The company also produces smaller quantities of zinc, lead, and copper.

The slide below is a corporate summary taken from the January Investor Presentation in which the company advertises itself as "The World's Premier Silver Mining Company":

Earnings

PAAS released its Q3FY25 earnings report on November 12th. As you might expect given the rally in silver and gold, it was a very bullish report. Highlights included:

  • Q3 revenue of 884.4 million was +19% yoy.
  • Q3 net earnings were $169.2 million ($0.45/share). EPS was +50% yoy.
  • Q3 free-cash-flow was a record $251.7 million, or an estimated $0.57/share based on the 422.04 million shares outstanding at quarter's end.
  • The Board approved another increase to the dividend, raising it to $0.14/share with respect to Q3 2025.

The following chart from the Q3 earnings release shows how the company is performing from an operational standpoint:

As you can see from the graphic, silver production was relatively flat yoy, gold production was -18.4% yoy, while relative to silver and gold production, the company's production of zinc, lead, and copper is non-important in my opinion.

The real action is, of course, in the realized prices category. As you can see, the company's realized prices of silver and gold were up 32.4% and 40.6%, respectively, yoy. With price increases like that, production can stay flat or even decline, and PAAS is still going to post very strong numbers (and it did).

It is also important to note that Q3 realized prices are significantly below Friday's close for both silver and gold: $103/oz and $4999/oz, respectively. Note: I no longer even bother with the digits to the right of the decimal point because they no longer matter to me given the size of the numbers to the left of the decimal point these days.

The other major news during PAAS's third quarter report was the closing of the MAG Silver acquisition. PAAS President & CEO Michael Steinmann commented on the impact to the company:

We completed our acquisition of MAG Silver on September 4, and with only a one-month contribution from our 44% interest in the Juanicipio mine, we are already seeing its significant contribution to our Silver Segment operations and free cash flow generation. Consequently, we are raising our 2025 silver production guidance and lowering our estimated all-in sustaining costs for the Silver Segment to reflect Juanicipio’s contribution to the portfolio. We are maintaining the remainder of our 2025 operating outlook, which continues to call for back-end weighted gold production.

So, PAAS shareholders can expect stronger silver production, back-end weighted gold production, and higher prices for each metal. It doesn't get any better than that, right?

Going Forward

Pan American Silver gave the following full-year 2025 guidance with the release of its Q3 results:

As you can see, compared to the annual guidance given in February 2025, the company significantly raised FY2025 silver production while significantly lowering the AISC price for silver (from a midpoint of $17.25/oz to a midpoint of $14.75/oz, or a reduction of 14.5%). The bottom line: higher production combined with a much lower cost per ounce of production.

Increased Stake in Galleon

However, since the Q3 EPA report and guidance were issued, Pan American Silver has since announced an "Early Warning News Release" on December 5, which reported an increase in the company's stake in Galleon Gold Corp (PNCKF) (GGO:CA):

... Pan American now owns, directly or indirectly, or exercises control or direction over, 18,750,000 Common Shares, 9,375,000 Warrants and the Debenture which represent approximately 14.7% of the total number of issued and outstanding Common Shares on a non-diluted basis and approximately 29.7% of the total number of issued and outstanding Common Shares on a partially diluted basis.

The bottom line here is that if Pan American converted the principal amount of its debenture and exercised all of its warrants, Pan American would own 45,902,777 Common Shares of Galleon, representing ~19.6% and ~29.7% of the common shares on a fully and partially diluted basis, respectively.

As you can see from Galleon Gold's January Presentation, the significance of PAAS's investment in the company is highlighted by the strategic location of the Prolific Timmins Mining Camp: the West Cache Project sits between major producers PAAS and Discovery Silver (DSV:TSX) and has multi-million-ounce potential over the near-term:

That being the case, the increase of PAAS's stake in Galleon did not come as much of a surprise given its previous stake in the company and the strong influx of cash PAAS has enjoyed, which has enabled it to easily expand its interests in Galleon. Both stocks responded positively since the announcement on Dec. 5th, which, given the continued rally in the prices of silver and gold, is arguably a relatively small development in comparison to PAAS's increased stake in Galleon:

The presentation says Galleon has a current market cap, including debentures, of C$240.5 million. Given PAAS's current market cap of $27.2 billion, it is certainly conceivable, should terms be auspicious, that PAAS could end up purchasing all of Galleon Gold.

2025 Production Numbers and 2026 Guidance

Also, just last week, Pan American Silver announced 2025 Production results and updated cash balances.

Highlights included:

  • Silver production of 22.8 million ounces exceeded the updated annual guidance range, with a record 7.3 million ounces produced in Q4 2025.
  • Juanicipio mine production exceeded expectations, contributing 2.5 million ounces of silver just since the mine was acquired in September 2025. The mine also posted a $44 million dividend that PAAS received in December 2025.
  • Estimated end-of-year cash and short-term investments totaled $1.319 billion (on an unaudited basis), representing an increase of a whopping ~$408 million from the end of Q3. Better still, that estimate excludes an additional $127 million of cash attributable to the company's 44% investment in Juanicipio.
  • Pan American Silver's revolving credit facility remained undrawn at year-end, resulting in total available liquidity estimated at $2.1 billion.

The announcement also gave guidance for 2026:

As compared to the previously announced 2025 production numbers:

  • Guidance for 2026 silver production is, at the midpoint, expected to increase by 14% (26 million oz compared to 22.8 million oz).
  • Guidance for 2026 gold production is, at the midpoint, expected to decline by ~2.3% compared to 2025 production (725,000 oz versus 742,200 oz in 2025).
  • The midpoint of silver AISC ($17/oz) would be $1/oz higher than the top-end of updated 2025 guidance. But - again - that is a relative non-event given the huge appreciation in the price of silver.
  • Ditto for the midpoint of gold AISC ($1,775/oz) - that's a $150/oz increase above the high-end of updated 2025 guidance, which, in my opinion, is not much of a concern given the huge increase in the price of gold itself.

Q4 Earnings Preview

PAAS is scheduled to release its Q4 and full-year 2025 results on February 18th, after the market closes. The current consensus earnings estimates courtesy of Yahoo Finance are shown below:

As you can see from the graphic, the average EPS estimate for Q4 is $0.88, which would be +151% on a yoy basis and +95.5% sequentially. The current average Q4 revenue estimate is for $1.12 billion, which, if accurate, would be up $235.6 million on a sequential basis (+26.6%).

While the estimates may appear rational given that at the end of Pan American Silver's Q4 (Dec. 31st), silver closed at $72.28/oz and gold closed at $4,334/oz, the revenue estimate is on the low-end of what I am expecting. I say that because the company already pre-released its estimated year-end cash position (see above), and it was a $408 million sequential increase, which is quite a large increase if revenue is only going to be up by the consensus estimate on a sequential basis (i.e., the $235.6 million figure above). As a result, and unless these estimates are updated soon, PAAS is likely to deliver a rather large beat on both the top- and bottom-lines, in my view. The analysts' revenue estimate looks like it could be as much as $100-150 million too low.

That said, and as good as Q4 earnings will likely be, the real fireworks for PAAS will likely start with the Q1 earnings release, which will encompass the massive YTD rally in both metals (and what happens from now until the end of March).

Dividends

As mentioned earlier, the outstanding share count of PAAS at the end of Q3 was 422.04 million. Shareholders should note that was up from 363.00 million in Q3 of 2024, primarily due to the impact of the MAG Silver acquisition. The point here is that increased dividends are now obligated to an outstanding share count that has risen by 16.3% over the past year. Yet despite the share dilution, as also pointed out earlier, free-cash-flow in Q3 was still an estimated $0.57/share, which very easily covered the new and increased $0.14/share dividend. Clearly then, and combined with the rapidly increasing price of both silver and gold, PAAS shareholders should continue to see significant increases in the dividend going forward.

Speaking of that, Pan American Silver's dividend policy is stated as follows:

"Pan American’s dividend policy supplements a base dividend of $0.10 per common share with a variable dividend, paid on a quarterly basis, that is linked to the net cash on the balance sheet for the previous quarter, as illustrated in the following table:"

According to PAAS's recent 2025 update referenced above, the company's year-end cash position was $1.319 billion, while the Q3 presentation reported total debt of $857 million, primarily related to senior notes:

  • $283M with a 4.625% coupon maturing in December 2027
  • $500M with 2.63% coupon maturing in August 2031

From that information, my estimate is for a net-cash position of $462 million at the end of 2025, which from the chart above is a total dividend of $0.18/share - a $0.04/share increase over Q3.

However, the bigger issue here is that - in my opinion - the table above needs to be revised in order to take into account the massive increase in the prices of both silver and gold. Indeed, shareholders are now in an "off the chart" situation with respect to the dividends return policy. Specifically, PAAS management and the Board arguably need to add two more lines to the chart above: "$500 million or greater" and "$600 million or greater". Otherwise, shareholders will quite likely get short-changed on the dividends going forward in 2026. It will be interesting to hear if analysts question the company on this issue during the upcoming Q4 conference call, which is due to be held on Feb. 18th after the market closes.

Risks

The risks for PAAS shareholders are geopolitical, the strength (or, in this case, weakness...) of the U.S. dollar, which directly impacts the prices of silver and gold, mine operational risks, and tangential risks from the global macroeconomic environment (i.e., industrial and consumer buying of silver and central-bank buying of gold reserves).

Those are all the risks that can impact the prices of silver and gold, but at the end of the day, it is those prices that will determine (via the company's average realized price) the company's revenue and earnings. Look at it this way: the price of silver could fall 50% on Monday (i.e., to $51.96/oz), and that price would still be $12.08/oz about PAAS's realized price for Q3, a quarter in which PAAS delivered record FCF of $251.7 million.

PAAS's balance sheet is strong, is very rapidly strengthening given the quick escalation of silver and gold prices. The company has over $2 billion of available liquidity.

According to Yahoo Finance, PAAS has a TTM P/E=37.3x, and the forward P/E as reported by Seeking Alpha is currently 29x. However, neither the P/E ratio - in my opinion - take into account the massive price increases of both silver and gold since the end of Q3. As a result, these P/E ratios do not take into account the massive influx of free-cash-flow the company is likely to report in the upcoming Q4 earnings release. Yet, Seeking Alpha Quant dings the stock on the Valuation metric grade:

But, as you can see, the rest of the Factor Grades are very impressive. And I believe it is the "Profitability Factor" that is going to ultimately dominate the valuation of PAAS stock moving forward.

Summary & Conclusion

I am very bullish on Pan American Silver Corp. That's because recent events in Davos only reinforce what I have writing about on Seeking Alpha since early last year: it is my opinion that the unwise, erratic, chaotic, and non-strategic policies of the Trump administration have led to a rapid decline of American Exceptionalism, have unnecessarily injected unprecedented uncertainty into the global financial and security alliances, and as a result we will likely continue to experience a secular long-term bear market in the value of the U.S. dollar and a continued flight to safety. In fact, since early last year, I have written many Seeking Alpha comments opining that "gold was made for a president like Trump." That was arguably an understatement.

The bottom line is that this means continued appreciation in the value of silver and gold until something significantly changes in Washington, DC. And given that Trump appears to have a strong hold on both the House and Senate, as well as the Supreme Court, I think that means clear sailing for Pan American Silver in 2026 and beyond. That said, if the Democrats can somehow wrestle the House and Senate from the Republicans, that could be a game-changer and, in my opinion, could cause the U.S. dollar to strengthen because it would send a signal to the world that there are at least some Americans that are not happy with the Trump administration's trade and tariff policies and/or with the way the United States has been treating Canada, Greenland, and other founding members of NATO.

I will not put a price target on the shares of PAAS because the prices of gold and silver are rallying so fast I'd have to revise the target every few days. Indeed, as I finish up this article (Monday morning), gold is already moving in on $5100, and silver is up another few dollars an ounce.

PAAS is a Strong Buy.
